perturbation.original_text: EXCEPT FOR BREACHES OR VIOLATIONS OF ARTICLE 9, OR INDEMNITY LIABILITIES ARISING UNDER THIS ARTICLE 8, OR CASES OF GROSS NEGLIGENCE OR WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER FOR ANY CONSEQUENTIAL, INDIRECT, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES INCLUDING LOSS OF USE, REVENUES OR PROFITS, INTERRUPTION OF BUSINESS OR CLAIMS AGAINST EITHER PARTY OR ITS CUSTOMERS BY ANY THIRD PARTY, WHETHER SUCH CLAIM IS BASED IN CONTRACT, TORT (INCLUDING NEGLIGENCE), STRICT LIABILITY OR OTHERWISE, EVEN IF THE PARTY IS ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES.
perturbation.changed_text: IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER FOR ANY CONSEQUENTIAL, INDIRECT, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES INCLUDING LOSS OF USE, REVENUES OR PROFITS, INTERRUPTION OF BUSINESS OR CLAIMS AGAINST EITHER PARTY OR ITS CUSTOMERS BY ANY THIRD PARTY, WHETHER SUCH CLAIM IS BASED IN CONTRACT, TORT (INCLUDING NEGLIGENCE), STRICT LIABILITY OR OTHERWISE, EVEN IF THE PARTY IS ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception for 'breaches or violations of Article 9, or indemnity liabilities arising under this Article 8, or cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct', the modified text now attempts to limit liability even in cases of intentional misconduct or breach of confidentiality, which can be legally problematic and unconscionable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Texas DTPA prohibits waiving certain consumer rights. By attempting to limit liability even for intentional misconduct, the modified clause could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent the DTPA's protections against unconscionable actions. Specifically, section 17.42(c) implies that waivers must not be against public policy, and limiting liability for gross negligence may violate this.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: rson other than the owner is punishable by law, is visibly displayed on the container. (f) A person may not: (1) remove a shopping cart or laundry cart from the premises or parking area of a retail establishment with intent to temporarily or permanently deprive the owner of the cart or the retailer of possession of the cart; (2) remove a shopping cart or laundry cart, without written authorization from the owner of the cart, from the premises or parking area of any retail establishment; (3) possess, without the written permission of the owner or retailer in lawful possession of the cart, a shopping cart or laundry cart outside the premises or parking lot of the retailer whose name or mark appears on the cart; or (4) remove, obliterate, or alter a serial number, name, or mark affixed to a shopping cart or laundry cart. (g) The requiring, taking, or accepting of a deposit on delivery of a container, shopping cart, or laundry cart is not considered a sale of the container or cart. (h) A person who violates this section commits an offense. An offense under this section is a Class C misdemeanor. Each violation constitutes a separate offense. (i) This section does not apply to the owner of a shopping cart, laundry cart, or container or to a customer or any other person who has written consent from the owner of a shopping cart, laundry cart, or container or from a retailer in lawful possession of the cart or container to remove it from the premises or the parking area of the retail establishment. For the purposes of this section, the term "written consent" includes tokens and other indicia of consent established by the owner of the carts or the retailer. Added by Acts 1989, 71st Leg., ch. 724, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1989. SUBCHAPTER E. DECEPTIVE TRADE PRACTICES AND CONSUMER PROTECTION Sec. 17.41. SHORT TITLE. This subchapter may be cited as the Deceptive Trade Practices-Consumer Protection Act. Added by Acts 1973, 63rd Leg., p. 322, ch. 143, Sec. 1, eff. May 21, 1973. Sec. 17.42. WAIVERS: PUBLIC POLICY. (a) Any waiver by a consumer of the provisions of this subchapter is contrary to public policy and is unenforceable and void; provided, however, that a waiver is valid and enforceable if: (1) the waiver is in writing and is signed by the consumer; (2) the consumer is not in a significantly disparate bargaining position; and (3) the consumer is represented by legal counsel in seeking or acquiring the goods or services. (b) A waiver under Subsection (a) is not effective if the consumer's legal counsel was directly or indirectly identified, suggested, or selected by a defendant or an agent of the defendant. (c) A waiver under this section must be: (1) conspicuous and in bold-face type of at least 10 points in size; (2) identified by the heading "Waiver of Consumer Rights," or words of similar meaning; and (3) in substantially the following form: "I waive my rights under the Deceptive Trade Practices-Consumer Protection Act, Section 17.41 et seq., Business & Commerce Code, a law that gives consumers special rights and protections. After consultation with an attorney of my own selection, I voluntarily consent to this waiver." (d) The waiver required by Subsection (c) may be modified to waive only specified rights under this subchapter. (e) The fact that a consumer has signed a waiver under this section is not a defense to an action brought by the attorney general under Section 17.47 . Added by Acts 1973, 63rd Leg., p. 322, ch. 143, Sec. 1, eff. May 21, 1973. Amended by Acts 1981, 67th Leg., p. 863, ch. 307, Sec. 1, eff. Aug. 31, 1981; Acts 1983, 68th Leg., p. 4943, ch. 883, Sec. 1, eff. Aug. 29, 1983; Acts 1987, 70th Leg., ch. 167, Sec. 5.02(6), eff. Sept. 1, 1987; Acts 1989, 71st Leg., ch. 380, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1989; Acts 1995, 74th Leg., ch. 414, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1995. Sec. 17.43. CUMULATIVE REMEDIES. The provisions of this subchapter are not exclusive. The remedies provided in this subchapter are in addition to any other procedures

perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes exceptions for breaches of Article 9, indemnity liabilities, gross negligence, or willful misconduct, which the original contract explicitly preserved. The Law Explanation states that limiting liability for gross negligence may violate the Texas DTPA, particularly section 17.42(c), which implies waivers must not be against public policy. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Sec. 17.42 (a), reinforces that waivers of consumer rights are against public policy and unenforceable unless specific conditions are met, such as being in writing, signed by the consumer, and the consumer being represented by legal counsel. By removing the exceptions, the modified text broadens the liability waiver beyond what is legally permissible under the DTPA, creating a direct contradiction. The omission directly undermines consumer protections intended by the DTPA.

perturbation.original_text: ESTABLISHMENT, at its sole cost and expense, will maintain appropriate insurance including, but not limited to, Commercial General Liability Insurance with premises, operations coverage including Person Injury/Property Damage coverage, with limits of not less than $1,000,000 per occurrence. As of January 1, 2015, such insurance shall also have annual aggregate limits not less than $2,000,000. Evidence of insurance indicating such coverage will be delivered to APOLLO upon request. The evidence will (a) indicate that the policy will not change or terminate without at least fifteen (15) days prior written notice to APOLLO, (b) APOLLO shall be listed as an additional insured on the commercial general liability policy.
perturbation.changed_text: ESTABLISHMENT, at its sole cost and expense, will maintain appropriate insurance. Evidence of insurance may be delivered to APOLLO. APOLLO may be listed as an additional insured.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies minimum insurance coverage and notification requirements. The modified clause removes the specific coverage limits, the annual aggregate limits, the requirement for 15-day notice of policy changes or termination, and makes APOLLO being listed as additional insured discretionary. This reduction in required insurance coverage and the removal of APOLLO's protection as an additional insured increases APOLLO's risk and reduces ESTABLISHMENT's accountability. This contradicts standard risk management practices and may violate implied duties of reasonable care.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (Texas)
perturbation.law_citation: Texas Law Regarding Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While Texas does not recognize a universal implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, it may be implied in certain contracts, especially those involving special relationships. Even without a special relationship, drastically reducing insurance coverage, failing to ensure sufficient coverage, and removing APOLLO as an additional insured may be viewed as acting in bad faith and undermining the purpose of the contract, creating risk for APOLLO that is not reasonable or expected.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates specific insurance coverage limits and notification requirements to APOLLO, and the changed text removes these protections. This removal directly contradicts the principle of maintaining reasonable risk management and ensuring adequate coverage, as the changed text allows for minimal insurance and discretionary additional insured status, undermining APOLLO's protection. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this change increases APOLLO's risk and reduces ESTABLISHMENT's accountability, which is a clear shift away from reasonable care. The Law Explanation emphasizes that even in the absence of a universal duty of good faith, drastically reducing insurance coverage can be viewed as acting in bad faith, which further supports the existence of a contradiction. While the scraped snippets are not directly relevant, the combined analysis of the original and changed texts, coupled with the Perturbation and Law Explanations, strongly indicate that a legal contradiction exists because core legal obligations have been removed.

perturbation.original_text: Any dispute arising under or relating to this Agreement shall be submitted for resolution to a state or federal court of competent jurisdiction in Austin, Texas, and the Parties hereby agree to submit to the jurisdiction and venue of such court.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute arising under or relating to this Agreement shall be resolved through binding arbitration, waiving all rights to litigation.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies that disputes will be resolved in a court of law in Austin, Texas. The modified clause mandates binding arbitration, forcing both parties to waive their right to sue and have a trial in a court of law. While arbitration is generally legal, mandatory arbitration clauses are sometimes scrutinized, especially if they are deemed unfair or unconscionable.
perturbation.law_citation: Texas Civil Practice and Remedies Code § 171.001
perturbation.law_explanation: The Texas Arbitration Act permits arbitration agreements, but courts can refuse to enforce them if they are unconscionable or induced by fraud. By mandating binding arbitration without explicitly addressing fairness, access to information, or cost-sharing, the modified clause risks being challenged as unconscionable, particularly if one party has significantly less bargaining power or access to resources. This contradiction is an attempt to preemptively waive consumer rights under Texas law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE CHAPTER 171. GENERAL ARBITRATION CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE TITLE 7. ALTERNATE METHODS OF DISPUTE RESOLUTION CHAPTER 171. GENERAL ARBITRATION SUBCHAPTER A. GENERAL PROVISIONS Sec. 171.001. ARBITRATION AGREEMENTS VALID. (a) A written agreement to arbitrate is valid and enforceable if the agreement is to arbitrate a controversy that: (1) exists at the time of the agreement; or (2) arises between the parties after the date of the agreement. (b) A party may revoke the agreement only on a ground that exists at law or in equity for the revocation of a contract. Acts 1965, 59th Leg., p. 1593, ch. 689, Sec. 1, eff. Jan. 1, 1966. Amended by Acts 1979, 66th Leg., p. 1708, ch. 704, Sec. 1, eff. Aug. 27, 1979. Redesignated from Vernon's Ann.Civ.Stat. art. 224 and amended by Acts 1995, 74th Leg., ch. 588, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1995. Amended by Acts 1997, 75th Leg., ch. 165, Sec. 5.01, eff. Sept. 1, 1997. The following section was amended by the 89th Legislature. Pending publication of the current statutes, see H.B. 40 , 89th Legislature, Regular Session, for amendments affecting the following section. Sec. 171.002. SCOPE OF CHAPTER. (a) This chapter does not apply to: (1) a collective bargaining agreement between an employer and a labor union; (2) an agreement for the acquisition by one or more individuals of property, services, money, or credit in which the total consideration to be furnished by the individual is not more than $50,000, except as provided by Subsection (b); (3) a claim for personal injury, except as provided by Subsection (c); (4) a claim for workers' compensation benefits; or (5) an agreement made before January 1, 1966. (b) An agreement described by Subsection (a)(2) is subject to this chapter if: (1) the parties to the agreement agree in writing to arbitrate; and (2) the agreement is signed by each party and each party's attorney. (c) A claim described by Subsection (a)(3) is subject to this chapter if: (1) each party to the claim, on the advice of counsel, agrees in writing to arbitrate; and (2) the agreement is signed by each party and each party's attorney. Acts 1965, 59th Leg., p. 1593, ch. 689, Sec. 1, eff. Jan. 1, 1966. Redesignated fro
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies dispute resolution in a court of law, while the modified text mandates binding arbitration, effectively waiving the right to litigation. The Texas Civil Practice and Remedies Code, Section 171.001(a), states that a written agreement to arbitrate is valid and enforceable. However, 171.001(b) notes that a party may revoke the agreement on grounds that exist at law or in equity for the revocation of a contract. The LLM explanation correctly highlights that mandatory arbitration without fairness considerations can be challenged as unconscionable, especially if one party has less bargaining power. While the law snippet affirms the validity of arbitration agreements generally, the omission of fairness considerations in the modified clause introduces a potential legal vulnerability. Therefore, by removing the court option and imposing mandatory arbitration without addressing potential fairness issues, the changed text creates a legally questionable situation regarding the enforceability of the agreement, thus creating a contradiction.
